topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon was dull, the kind of gray that seeped into the edges of the room and settled on my shoulders like damp wool. I was just retrieving junk mail from the overflowing slot when my fingers brushed against something thick and creamy tucked beneath a flyer for a local bake sale. It was a card, folded precisely, bearing no address, no name, just a single, elegant script wishing a 'Happy Birthday' to an unnamed recipient. My thumb traced the embossed lettering, a strange warmth spreading through my palm. I turned it over, the paper feeling expensive, and saw nothing but blankness. I stood there for a full minute, the mundane sounds of traffic outside seeming distant, as if I were observing something entirely out of sync. Who had sent this? And why? A knot tightened in my chest, a sudden, inexplicable pull toward a story I couldn't access.

[story 2]

I picked up the card with two fingers, the texture surprisingly smooth against my skin. It was pristine, the edges sharp, and the message inside was a short, cryptic poem about forgotten stars. I sat down heavily on the worn armchair, the paper trembling slightly in my grip. I reread the words three times, searching for a clue, a signature, any indication of origin. Nothing. It was as if the card existed in a vacuum, completely detached from any context. A slow, almost involuntary shiver ran down my spine. I looked around my apartment, at the familiar clutter, and suddenly everything felt layered, concealing secrets I hadn't known existed. My breath hitched, shallow and quick.

[story 3]

The mail slot always offered the same predictable assortment of bills and advertisements, but this was different. Tucked between a utility bill and a glossy magazine was a card. It was unsigned, the paper a pale ivory, and the sentiment—a simple, heartfelt birthday wish—felt profoundly misplaced. I held it up to the light, examining the faint impression of the handwriting. It was beautiful, yet utterly alien. My jaw went slack. I didn't move for several long seconds, my eyes fixed on the card as if it held the key to a hidden door. A faint tremor started in my hands, and I slowly, deliberately, slid the card into my wallet, the action feeling momentous, as if I were safeguarding a fragile, unknown artifact.
